title: Evidence Revision Profile

introduction.subtitle: How firmly do you hold beliefs — and what would change them? Four axes of the tension between conviction and openness.
introduction.description:
For each of twelve statements you do two things: rate your agreement, then say what would change your mind. This is not about whether you are right; it maps the tension between how strongly you hold a belief and how open you are to revising it.

Inspired by actively open-minded thinking (AOT) scales. Original items.

Use the result as language for reflection and conversation, not as a permanent label. The terms are meant to make patterns easier to notice, compare, and discuss.
introduction.duration: 12 statements, two answers each. Approximately five minutes.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is your choice.
introduction.disclaimer: Inspired by actively open-minded thinking (AOT) scales (public domain). Items are original adaptations. This is not a validated psychometric assessment.

questions.text: Market incentives usually produce better outcomes than centralized planning.

conceptHelp.title: Conviction strength: Market incentives usually produce better outcomes than centralized planning.
conceptHelp.summary: Conviction strength means how strongly this exact claim currently feels true to you: "Market incentives usually produce better outcomes than centralized planning.". The quiz asks this before it asks what evidence could move your view.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Separating conviction from revision makes the result more useful: a strong belief can still be open to evidence, and a weak belief can still be held carelessly.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from your present view today. Do not soften the rating to seem open-minded and do not harden it to seem certain.
conceptHelp.example: A high rating means this exact statement feels convincing to you now; a low rating means you doubt it, reject it, or think it is too broad.

questions.text: What would change your mind about the statement above?

options.text: Multiple independent, peer-reviewed studies showing the opposite.
One credible counterexample from a source I trust.
I would reconsider how I apply it, but not the principle itself.
Nothing would change my mind on this.

conceptHelp.title: Evidence revision: what would change your mind about "Market incentives usually produce better outcomes than centralized planning."
conceptHelp.summary: Evidence revision means naming the observation, argument, result, or lived example that would make you update a belief. Here the belief is "Market incentives usually produce better outcomes than centralized planning.".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: A useful answer distinguishes real openness from the vague claim that you are open-minded. It shows what would actually count as counter-evidence.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Ask what new information would genuinely move you: repeated data, a failed prediction, a better causal explanation, expert disagreement, or a concrete counterexample.
conceptHelp.example: A strong response names the evidence that would change the claim "Market incentives usually produce better outcomes than centralized planning." instead of saying that nothing could change it.

attribution.text: Inspired by actively open-minded thinking (AOT) scales (public domain). Items are original adaptations.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
